issues.title: ToolCallback fails if tool context is provided

comments.body: @filiphr Thanks very much for the detailed write-up reporting the issue!

Introducing `ToolContextAwareToolCallback` sounds like a clean architectural solution, but I think it comes with widespread changes across multiple classes.

 .  . .

@filiphr 

You're right — I misunderstood that part. Appreciate the clarification.

@filiphr While your design approach provides a path to address the use case suggested, we propose this PR: https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-ai/pull/3784 where we can avoid throwing exception but logging a debug message. 

We believe this fixes the underlying problem where we shouldn't be throwing an exception at first place. Please review and let us know your feedback. Thanks!
Thanks @ilayaperumalg, PR #3784 indeed fixes the underlying problem with the exception throwing. I've reviewed the PR and I've left a comment there.

issues.title: Unstable MCP implementation for reactive webflux client (IntelliJ MCP not working)
issues.body:
**Bug description**

When using webflux client the MCP usage is unstable. It works for some MCPs and it doesn't work for others.
For instance it woks for [Vaadin MCP](https://github.com/marcushellberg/vaadin-documentation-services) but not for [IntelliJ MCP](https://github.com/JetBrains/mcp-jetbrains/blob/main/README.md#configuration). 

Important to mention that at some moment during last week the Anthropic case was working. 

**Environment**
- Spring AI 1.0.0-SNAPSHOT (2025-05-12)
- Java 17
- IntelliJ 2025.1 UE
- IntelliJ MCP Server 1.0.29

**Steps to reproduce**
- Install IntelliJ and IntelliJ MCP server plugin and open it with any maven project (it can be another type of project but the provided test query asks for pom file information)
- clone [this example application ](https://github.com/mgarciavaadin/test-mcp-reactive)
- Setup ANTHROPIC_API_KEY or/and OPENAI_API_KEY environment variables with your API keys
- Run TestMcpApplication
- In terminal run 

To test IntelliJ MCP 
```script
 curl "http://localhost:8080/chat?userInput=List%20project%20dependencies%20on%20pom%20file"
```
To test Vaadin MCP 
```script
 curl "http://localhost:8080/chat?userInput=What%20do%20you%20know%20about%20Vaadin%20Dashbord%20component"
```
- In main branch using standard client everything works
- In reactive branch using webflux and Anthropic 
-- Vaadin MCP still works (you will see updated information of Vaadin Dashboard new released component)
-- IntelliJ MCP does not work. It stops before calling the tool and no exception is thrown. (You will see that the agent tries to access the tool but it stops there. Something like 
```script
I'll help you list the project dependencies from the pom.xml file. Let me first check if there's a pom.xml file in your project.I found a pom.xml file in your project. Let me retrieve its content to show you the dependencies:
``` 
- In reactive-openai branch using webflux and OpenAI
-- Vaadin MCP and IntelliJ MCP do not work. It throws a bad request exception. 

Weird thing is that if you only setup Vaadin MCP, for openAI case it works. It is only after setting up IntelliJ MCP when it fails. For Anthropic it still works. You can test modifying src/main/resources/mcp-servers-config.json
  
**Expected behavior**
The expected behavior is the same that you get with the standard client. In the case of this test this is some information of the pom file of the project. 

**Minimal Complete Reproducible example**
Provided in the [example application ](https://github.com/mgarciavaadin/test-mcp-reactive)

comments.body:
Not solving the whole issue but related to OpenAI with IntelliJ MCP bad request topic. It looks like that the problem is with functions that don't have parameters. Loading the IntelliJ MCP generates tools with input schema as 
```json
{"type":"object"}
```
This generates the bad request. If these tools are completed with other parameters then the bad request disappear. 
```json
{"type":"object","properties":{},"required":[]}
```
Not sure if it is a wrong IntelliJ MCP implementation (non standard) or an issue on Spring AI tool loading from the MCP though. 

Hope it helps.

issues.title: Tools MethodToolCallback， parameter name cannot be matched

comments.body: on the MCP server and define a tools method with two parameters [public ComboDto getCombo(String name, String value)]. I use the MCP client to simulate the call, but the server cannot obtain the parameter value(name/value is null). I checked the source code because when the source code reflects, it is obtained based on the parameter name [Object rawArgument=toolInputArguments. get (parameter. getName())]

> on the MCP server and define a tools method with two parameters [public ComboDto getCombo(String name, String value)]. I use the MCP client to simulate the call, but the server cannot obtain the parameter value(name/value is null). I checked the source code because when the source code reflects, it is obtained based on the parameter name [Object rawArgument=toolInputArguments. get (parameter. getName())]

I ran into the same problem. When I used the log.info(t.getToolDefinition().inputSchema()) tool to print the mcp server function's schema information, I noticed it only showed `arg0` and `arg1` instead of `name` and`value`. So after changing the parameter keys to `arg0` and `arg1`, the arguments were passed successfully.

@lianneli 
I debug code find `String parameterName = method.getParameters()[i].getName() ` get parameterName  is **arg0**
[https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-ai/blob/v1.0.0-M6/spring-ai-core/src/main/java/org/springframework/ai/util/json/schema/JsonSchemaGenerator.java#L126](url)

so I set maven-compiler-plugin parameters true in my project and solve the problem
```               <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId>
                <artifactId>maven-compiler-plugin</artifactId>
                <version>3.6.2</version>
                <configuration>
                    <parameters>true</parameters>
                </configuration>
```

I think **@ToolParam**  need add **name** or **value**  such as **@RequestParam** in Spring MVC.
Method parameter name information is not preserved during compilation. Perhaps this can help.

Thanks everyone for the discussion — the workaround using `<parameters>true>` is helpful.

I'd like to contribute a proper fix for this by introducing a `@ToolParam("name")` annotation, similar to `@RequestParam` in Spring MVC. This would allow explicit parameter binding without relying on the compiler flag, and improve clarity and reliability.

I'll start working on a PR for this shortly.

cc: @leelance @lianneli @MrDjoy @Ballen2270 @tzolov
